pattern: The number of trips completed by a given time is a non-decreasing function of that time, so the smallest sufficient time can be pinned down with a binary search instead of simulating the buses minute by minute.
hints: For any fixed candidate amount of time, you can compute exactly how many total trips all buses would have completed by then without simulating anything -- it's a sum of integer divisions.
That total-trips-by-time function only ever goes up as time increases, which is exactly the property that makes binary search on the answer valid.
Binary search over candidate times: at each candidate, sum floor(time / time[i]) across all buses, and narrow the range based on whether that sum has reached totalTrips yet.

edge_inputs.description: single fastest-possible bus, one trip requested
single bus, a large number of trips requested
one very slow bus barely contributes while a fast bus does almost all the work
the required time lands exactly on a trip completion instant for both buses
buses at maximum time value with a large trip requirement

stress.description: random bus times and a random trip target
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    time = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    total_trips = rng.randint(1, 10000000)
    return [time, total_trips]
